old_claim.note: Harbor Lighthouses provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: A lighthouse system or lineage is composed of harbor lighthouse towers and beacon fires, with the Pharos serving as the archetype for later lighthouses.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica identifies the Pharos as the most famous lighthouse in antiquity and archetype for later lighthouses; it stood on Pharos island in Alexandria harbor, was finished about 280 BCE, and had a fire burning at night.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Britannica anchors the ancient lighthouse lineage in a harbor tower with an elevated nighttime fire at Alexandria.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The node is now scoped as a lighthouse lineage/system, so actual harbor lighthouse towers and beacon fires are the constituent units rather than optional context.

ontology_before: Lighthouse Networks treated Harbor Lighthouses as a generic enabling context.
ontology_after: Lighthouse Networks requires harbor lighthouse towers as the constituent technology in the ancient lighthouse lineage.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: lighthouse_networks -> harbor_lighthouses is now a required edge.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to non-lighthouse signal-fire chains rather than lighthouse systems.
A stronger source shows the ancient network category did not depend on harbor lighthouse towers.
A more precise ancient_lighthouse_lineage node is created and replaces this node.
short_reason: Lighthouse systems are composed of lighthouse towers and beacon fires.
